documents.title: Account Password and Sign-In Security
documents.content: Users can reset a forgotten password from the sign-in page by selecting Forgot password, entering the email address associated with the account, and following the secure reset link sent by email. The link expires after thirty minutes and can be used only once. Users who are already signed in can change their password from Settings > Security > Password. Passwords must be at least twelve characters long and should include a mix of letters, numbers, and symbols. Administrators cannot view existing passwords, but they can send a password reset email from the Admin Console. If a user reports that they did not receive the reset email, they should check spam filtering, confirm that the email address is active, and ask an administrator to verify that the account has not been deactivated. After five failed sign-in attempts, the account is temporarily locked for fifteen minutes. Multi-factor authentication can be enabled from Settings > Security > Multi-factor authentication. When MFA is enabled, users must provide a time-based code from an authenticator app after entering their password. Recovery codes should be downloaded and stored securely because support cannot recover them. If all MFA methods are lost, an organization administrator must verify the user's identity and reset MFA from the Admin Console.

documents.title: Billing, Plans, and Invoices
documents.content: Workspace owners can manage subscriptions from Billing > Plan. The Starter plan includes five seats, basic analytics, email support, and ten thousand document indexing credits per month. The Growth plan includes unlimited viewer seats, advanced analytics, priority support, and one hundred thousand indexing credits per month. The Enterprise plan adds custom retention settings, single sign-on, audit log export, dedicated support, and negotiated indexing limits. A seat is counted when a member has editor, analyst, or administrator access. Viewer-only users are not billable on Growth and Enterprise plans. Invoices are generated on the first day of each billing cycle and can be downloaded from Billing > Invoices. To update the payment method, a workspace owner should open Billing > Payment method and add a new card or bank account. Failed payments trigger email notifications to workspace owners. If payment is not resolved within seven days, the workspace enters a restricted state where existing data remains readable but new indexing jobs are paused. Plan downgrades take effect at the next renewal date. When a downgrade would exceed the destination plan limits, owners must remove extra seats or reduce indexed document volume before the change can be scheduled.

documents.title: Document Ingestion and Indexing
documents.content: The platform supports document ingestion from uploaded PDF files, Markdown files, plain text files, and approved cloud storage connectors. During ingestion, each document is parsed into normalized text, cleaned to remove repeated headers and footers, split into overlapping chunks, and embedded for semantic retrieval. Recommended chunk size is three hundred to five hundred tokens with a small overlap so that concepts spanning paragraph boundaries remain searchable. Each chunk stores metadata including the source document, document title, page number when available, chunk identifier, ingestion timestamp, and access policy. Indexing jobs are asynchronous. Users can monitor status from Knowledge Base > Indexing Jobs, where each job is shown as queued, processing, completed, or failed. Failed jobs include an error reason and can be retried after the source issue is corrected. Common failures include encrypted PDFs, unsupported file types, connector authorization expiry, and documents that exceed the workspace size limit. Re-indexing a document replaces old vectors for that document while preserving the document identifier. Deleted documents are removed from search results after the deletion event is processed. For best retrieval quality, documents should use descriptive headings, short sections, and factual language.

documents.title: Public API Usage and Rate Limits
documents.content: Developers can access the public API by creating an API key from Settings > Developer > API Keys. API keys are scoped to a workspace and inherit the permissions of the user who created them. Keys should be stored in a server-side secret manager and must never be embedded in browser code or mobile applications. The API accepts JSON requests and returns JSON responses. All requests must include an Authorization header using the Bearer token scheme. The default rate limit is six hundred requests per minute for read endpoints and one hundred requests per minute for write endpoints. Enterprise customers may request higher limits. When a client exceeds the limit, the API returns HTTP 429 with a retry-after header. Clients should use exponential backoff and avoid retrying non-idempotent write requests unless an idempotency key is supplied. Pagination uses cursor tokens rather than page numbers. The response body contains a nextCursor field when more results are available. API keys can be rotated by creating a replacement key, updating the application configuration, and revoking the old key after traffic has moved. Audit logs record key creation, revocation, and high-risk administrative API actions.

documents.title: Privacy, Retention, and Data Governance
documents.content: Customer documents are used only to provide workspace features such as indexing, retrieval, summarization, and access-controlled search. The service stores document text, embeddings, metadata, user account details, billing records, and operational logs. Workspace administrators can configure retention rules on Enterprise plans from Admin Console > Governance > Retention. A retention rule can delete documents, chat transcripts, or audit events after a specified period. Deleted documents are removed from the active knowledge base immediately and are purged from backups according to the backup retention schedule. Chat transcripts are retained for ninety days by default unless a workspace has configured a shorter or longer policy. Administrators can export audit logs to a security information and event management system. Data access is restricted by role-based permissions and, when enabled, document-level access policies. Support engineers may access diagnostic metadata during a support case, but they do not access customer document content without explicit approval. The platform encrypts data in transit using TLS and encrypts stored documents, embeddings, and database backups at rest. Customers can request a data processing addendum and region-specific hosting on Enterprise plans.

documents.title: Search and Chat Troubleshooting
documents.content: If the assistant returns an answer that appears incomplete, users should first check whether the relevant document has been indexed successfully. Indexing status is available in Knowledge Base > Indexing Jobs. A completed job means the document text was parsed, chunked, embedded, and added to the vector index. If a document is missing from search results, confirm that the user has permission to access it and that the document has not been deleted or excluded by a governance rule. Retrieval quality depends on the wording of the question and the quality of the source material. Questions that include specific product names, settings names, dates, or workflow details usually retrieve better context than vague questions. If chat answers cite outdated content, re-index the source document after updating it. Administrators can review retrieval diagnostics that show matched chunks, similarity scores, and source documents. Low similarity scores indicate that the knowledge base may not contain enough information to answer the question. In that case, the assistant should say that it could not find enough information instead of guessing. Browser issues can often be resolved by refreshing the page, starting a new chat session, or clearing local storage if the session identifier has become corrupted.
